author: DeleteAndRebuild
draft_comment:
The hidden insight here isn't about code—it's about deleting your mental model, not just your files.

When you refactor incrementally, you're constantly negotiating with your past decisions. Each line of legacy code carries embedded assumptions about what the system is, what it should do, and what's possible. You're not just fixing bugs—you're fighting gravity.

When you delete and rebuild, you're not starting fresh on the problem. You're starting fresh on *your understanding* of the problem. Each iteration of your rebuild wasn't just cleaner code—it was a cleaner *mind*.

That's why this worked: You didn't just delete code 3 times. You deleted the assumptions that led to that code. The 10x gain didn't come from better abstractions or patterns—it came from a better mental model.

Most "rewrite from scratch" efforts fail because people keep the same mental model and just change the syntax. You succeeded because each rebuild was a fresh perspective on what the system should be.

The pattern: Delete → Learn → Rebuild with better mental model → Repeat. That's not refactoring. That's compound interest on understanding.
